items.text: How do dietary fiber interventions alter gut microbiome composition and function in ways that modulate systemic immune responses, particularly in autoimmune and inflammatory disease contexts?
What microbial metabolites (short-chain fatty acids, secondary bile acids, tryptophan derivatives) mediate gut-brain axis communication, and how do they influence neurological disease risk and mood?

High dietary fiber increases Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus species, which produce butyrate and other SCFAs that strengthen the intestinal epithelial barrier and dampen pro-inflammatory cytokine signaling.
Loss of microbiome diversity in industrialized populations is causally linked to rising prevalence of non-communicable diseases including obesity, type 2 diabetes, and inflammatory bowel disease.

High-fiber diets consistently increase fiber-fermenting taxa (Faecalibacterium prausnitzii, Roseburia inulinivorans) and decrease Firmicutes-to-Bacteroidetes ratio; effects wane when fiber intake is discontinued.
FMT from lean donors temporarily improves insulin sensitivity in metabolic syndrome patients, but effects wane within weeks without dietary changes, suggesting diet shapes the engraftment niche.

Akkermansia muciniphila abundance inversely correlates with metabolic syndrome markers; pasteurized Akkermansia supplementation improves metabolic parameters in human trials without requiring viable bacteria.
